There's a real case for faster timelines too—AI is accelerating its own research, test-time compute opened new scaling possibilities, and METR's finding that task-horizon length is doubling every seven months suggests rapid progress on agency. But there are also genuine obstacles: hallucination and reliability issues persist, continual learning remains unsolved, and the definitional bar for AGI is high. I'll settle on roughly 20% as my headline estimate.
rows.answer:
Short answer up front: **~20%.**

Here's how I get there, because the number depends heavily on how you decompose it.

## The question is really a conjunction

P(AGI via LLMs by 2035) = P(AGI by 2035) × P(it's LLM-based | AGI by 2035)

These two factors pull in opposite directions, which is what makes the headline number moderate rather than extreme.

## Factor 1: P(AGI by 2035) ≈ 30%

Using a demanding definition (systems that can do the large majority of cognitive knowledge-work at or above competent-human level, including learning new tasks):

**Pushing higher:**
- Genuinely fast progress 2020→2025 (GPT-3 → reasoning models)
- A new scaling axis opened up (test-time compute / reasoning)
- METR's finding that the length of tasks AI can complete is doubling roughly every ~7 months — if that holds, long-horizon agency improves a lot by 2035
- Hundreds of billions in capital and compute committed
- The "AI accelerating AI research" feedback loop is no longer hypothetical

**Pushing lower:**
- Persistent unsolved problems: reliability/hallucination, continual learning, robust long-horizon agency
- Possible diminishing returns to pretraining scaling
- "AGI is 20 years away" has been true for ~70 years
- The remaining gaps may be qualitatively harder than the gains scaling already harvested

I land around **30%**, with a defensible range of 20–40%. Lab leaders would say much higher (50%+); superforecasters and skeptics much lower (<15%).

## Factor 2: P(LLM-based | AGI by 2035) ≈ 70%

This is the part Marcus gets backwards, in my view. **The shorter the timeline, the more likely the current paradigm wins** — simply because a fundamentally new architecture would need to be invented, scaled, and proven within the window, while all the talent, capital, and infrastructure is pointed at transformer/self-supervised-learning descendants.

The main caveat is definitional. Today's frontier systems are already not "pure LLMs" — they have RL post-training, tool use, agentic scaffolding, multimodality, reasoning. If AGI emerges from that lineage (even heavily modified), most people would call it "LLM-based." The credible "fundamentally different" contenders that could mature by 2035 are mostly world-model approaches (LeCun's JEPA line) — still deep learning, but a real departure. Neuro-symbolic systems usually *incorporate* LLMs rather than replace them.

I put this at **~70%**, range 60–85% depending on how strictly you define "LLM."

## Combining

0.30 × 0.70 ≈ **0.21**

## Bottom line: ~20%

A few things this implies:
- Marcus's "game over" is overconfident — declaring the dominant paradigm dead while it's still the single most likely path to AGI is a strong claim that the evidence doesn't support.
- But the "AGI is imminent via LLMs" camp is *also* overconfident — most of my probability mass (~80%) is on "not by 2035 via LLMs," driven mainly by the genuine chance AGI just doesn't arrive this decade.
- The estimate is most sensitive to Factor 1. If you believe P(AGI by 2035) is 50%, this becomes ~35%. If you think it's 15%, this becomes ~10%.

If I had to give one number: **~20%.**

**Short answer: roughly 25%.**

This is one of those questions where the honest work is in the decomposition, because the headline number is extremely sensitive to definitions. Let me show my reasoning.

## Two separate questions hiding inside one

The probability you're asking for is really a product:

**P(AGI via LLMs by 2035) = P(AGI of any kind by 2035) × P(it's LLM-lineage | AGI by 2035)**

### Factor 1: Will *any* form of AGI arrive by 2035?

This is the dominant and most contested term.

- **Bullish case:** Lab leaders (Amodei, Altman, much of DeepMind/OpenAI) put meaningful AGI in the late 2020s–early 2030s. Recent acceleration is real—reasoning models (o1/o3-style), agentic systems, fast benchmark saturation, and enormous capital deployment.
- **Bearish case:** Many serious researchers place human-level AI in the 2040s–2060s or treat it as open-ended. Aggregated expert surveys (e.g., AI Impacts) have been pulling *earlier* but still center well after 2035, with huge error bars. Marcus-style critiques—reliability, hallucination, brittle reasoning, poor sample efficiency, weak out-of-distribution generalization—have not been fully solved, only papered over.

My estimate: **~35%** for a genuinely broad, transformative AGI by 2035. Reasonable people span ~15% to ~55%.

### Factor 2: Conditional on AGI by 2035, is it LLM-lineage?

Here's the key insight: **a short timeline actually favors LLMs.** A fundamentally different architecture would need to be invented, matured, *and* overtake the colossal head start of transformers (in talent, compute, capital, tooling) in under a decade. That's a tall order. So if AGI arrives *fast*, it's most likely an evolution of today's paradigm.

My estimate: **~70–75%**, interpreting "LLM" generously as *transformer-based, large-pretrained systems—even when augmented with RL, tool use, memory, multimodality, and agentic scaffolding.*

## Combining

0.35 × 0.72 ≈ **~0.25**

## The big caveat: it's a definitions fight

- If "LLM" means *strictly* autoregressive text prediction with no architectural changes → much lower, because frontier systems already aren't that.
- If "fundamentally different" is interpreted to capture *any* major new ingredient (world models, neurosymbolic components, new training objectives), then a lot of plausible AGI paths get reclassified out of "LLM," dropping the number.
- Marcus's own framing assumes a crisp line that probably won't exist—the likely future is messy hybrids that both camps can claim as vindication.

On the Marcus post itself: "Game over" is rhetoric. His substantive point (pure scaling of next-token prediction may not be sufficient) is defensible; his confidence that it's *settled* is not, and he has a track record of premature obituaries.

**My single number: ~25%**, with a defensible range of roughly 15–35%. The biggest lever by far is your AGI-by-2035 timeline, not the architecture question.

rows.answer:
**Short answer: ~25%.**

The question is really a product of two sub-questions, and it's worth separating them because most of the disagreement (including Marcus's) lives in different places.

## Decomposition

**P(AGI achieved at all by 2035)** — call it ~35%

This depends heavily on definition. Using a demanding bar (matches human experts across most cognitive domains, with robust reasoning, long-horizon autonomy, and human-like learning of new tasks), I'd put this around 30–40%. Key tensions:

- *Bullish:* Benchmark saturation has been relentless; inference-time compute scaling (o1/o3-style) opened a new axis; enormous capital and talent concentration.
- *Bearish:* Current systems remain weak exactly where AGI is hard — reliability over long tasks, continual learning, genuine novelty/discovery, and not just pattern-matching benchmarks. Demanding definitions of AGI stress precisely these gaps.

**P(it's LLM-descended | AGI by 2035)** — call it ~70%

This is the crux Marcus is contesting, and I think he's *more likely wrong than right* conditional on short timelines, for a structural reason: **short timelines favor the incumbent paradigm.** A genuinely new architecture invented today would need ~5+ years to mature and scale (the transformer was 2017 and took years to dominate). If AGI shows up by 2035, the brute-force base rate favors something descended from today's transformer + RL + agentic-scaffolding lineage.

Counterweights pulling this below ~70%:
- A real **selection effect**: if LLMs *could* reach AGI, plausibly they'd do it earlier in the window. AGI arriving in ~2033 rather than ~2028 is mild evidence that new ingredients were needed.
- Serious researchers (LeCun's world-models/JEPA view, neurosymbolic camps) argue autoregressive LLMs are a dead end for true generality.
- **Definitional fuzziness:** future systems may absorb so many additions (world models, memory, neurosymbolic components, new training objectives) that whether they count as "an LLM" becomes a semantic fight. I'm treating "transformer-lineage + scaffolding" as still "via LLMs."

## Combining

~0.35 × ~0.70 ≈ **0.25**

## Honest caveats

- The number is dominated by the AGI-by-2035 term, which is the softest. Reasonable people span ~10% to ~45% on the overall question.
- Marcus's "LLMs are not the royal road" is a defensible claim about *architecture*, but his "Game over" framing conflates two things: (1) pure scaling of vanilla LLMs may be plateauing, and (2) the broad transformer-based paradigm is exhausted. The first is plausible; the second isn't yet supported — the field keeps finding new axes (RL on reasoning, tools, agents) on top of the same base.

So: **roughly 25%**, with most of my uncertainty being about whether *any* AGI arrives by 2035, not about which paradigm gets there first if one does.

rows.answer:
**Short answer: ~15%.**

Here's how I get there, because the number depends heavily on how you decompose it.

## Two definitional traps

**What counts as "AGI"?** This drives everything. If AGI means "outperforms most humans at most economically valuable cognitive work," the odds are much higher than if it means "matches expert humans across virtually all domains, with robust reasoning, continual learning, and reliable long-horizon autonomy." I'll use a demanding-but-not-maximal definition, roughly the latter minus the most extreme requirements.

**What counts as "an LLM"?** Frontier systems already aren't pure next-token predictors — they bundle RL, chain-of-thought/reasoning, tool use, retrieval, multimodality, and agentic scaffolding. I'll treat "LLM" as that whole *transformer-lineage foundation-model paradigm*, and "fundamentally different" as a genuinely new core (neurosymbolic à la Marcus, JEPA/world-model-first à la LeCun, neuromorphic, or something uninvented).

## Decomposition

I split this into two questions:

**1) P(AGI by 2035) ≈ 25%**
- *For higher:* Stunning 2022–2025 progress, massive capital, reasoning/test-time-compute as a new scaling axis, credible insider timelines (Amodei, Hassabis, Altman in the 2027–2032 range), and possible acceleration from AI automating AI research.
- *For lower:* "AGI" is a high bar that benchmarks flatter. Persistent unsolved problems — hallucination, brittleness, weak continual learning, unreliable long-horizon agency, poor sample efficiency — may be *hard* in a decades-not-years sense. Pretraining scaling shows diminishing returns. And AI has a long history of "10 years away."

I land around 25% — above skeptics, below lab leaders.

**2) P(it's LLM-lineage | AGI by 2035) ≈ 65%**
- A fundamentally new paradigm would need to be invented, matured, *and* scaled to AGI within ~10 years, overtaking a paradigm with enormous head start, infrastructure, and talent. That's hard.
- There's also a selection effect: if AGI arrives this *soon*, that's evidence the current paradigm was basically on track.
- Counterweight: the most plausible "different" architectures (world-model-centric, neurosymbolic hybrids) might absorb deep learning components, and the closer you look, the blurrier "LLM vs. not" becomes. The remaining failures could be exactly what forces a paradigm shift.

I put this around 65%.

**Joint: 0.25 × 0.65 ≈ 0.16 → ~15%.**

## What this does and doesn't say about Marcus

My 15% means I'm putting ~85% on "either no AGI by 2035, or it comes via something meaningfully different." But that does **not** vindicate the strong Marcus claim. The bulk of that 85% is *"AGI just hasn't arrived yet by 2035,"* not *"LLMs are intrinsically incapable."* His "game over" framing conflates "current systems have real limitations" (true) with "the paradigm is a dead end" (unproven). Over a longer horizon, the LLM-lineage share of AGI-paths rises.

## Sensitivity

- Weaker AGI definition → I'd move toward **25–35%**.
- If reasoning/agentic models keep clearing hard benchmarks *and* fixing reliability through 2026–2028 → **25–30%**.
- If scaling clearly plateaus and the hard problems prove stubborn → **5–10%**.

**Best single number: 15%.**

rows.answer:
**Best single-number estimate: ~30%.**

The key is to recognize this is really two questions multiplied together, and most of the uncertainty lives in the first one.

## Decomposition

**Factor 1 — Will AGI (broadly defined) arrive at all by 2035? ≈ 40%**

Using a demanding definition (an AI matching competent humans across essentially all cognitive domains, including reliable reasoning, continual learning, and long-horizon autonomy):

- *Pushing it up:* Unprecedented capital and compute, repeated capability surprises (GPT-3 → GPT-4 → reasoning/agentic models), and rapid gains on benchmarks once thought hard.
- *Pushing it down:* Ten years is short for the remaining gaps (robust out-of-distribution generalization, reliability/hallucination, grounded world models, genuine continual learning, durable long-horizon agency). AI has a long history of overoptimism, and pretraining scaling appears to be hitting diminishing returns.

I land around 40% — with a loose definition this could be 55%+, with a strict one closer to 25%.

**Factor 2 — *Given* AGI by 2035, is it LLM-descended rather than a fundamentally new architecture? ≈ 70%**

The shorter the timeline, the more this favors the incumbent paradigm. A genuinely new architecture would have to be invented, scaled, and overtake the enormous head start of transformer-based systems — all within a decade. That's possible but it's the harder path. More likely, novel ideas (world models, neurosymbolic components, new memory/RL schemes) get *absorbed into* the LLM lineage rather than replacing it.

**Joint: 0.40 × 0.70 ≈ 0.28 → ~30%.**

Note what the other ~70% is made of: most of it (~60%) is "AGI just doesn't arrive by 2035 at all," **not** "a different architecture beats LLMs to it" (~10%). So if you think AGI *is* coming this decade, you should also think LLMs are the most likely vehicle.

## The biggest caveat: definitions

Both terms are slippery, and your answer can swing 15+ points depending on how you set them:

- **"AGI"** — economically-useful broad competence vs. true human-equivalent-or-better generality are very different bars.

rows.answer:
**Short answer: ~20–25%. My single best estimate is 0.22.**

Here's how I get there, since the number is mostly driven by how you decompose it.

## Key definitional issue (matters more than it seems)

The biggest source of disagreement isn't forecasting—it's what counts as each category:

- **"AGI"**: I'm using a fairly demanding bar—matching competent humans across most economically valuable cognitive tasks, including reliability, long-horizon planning, and continual learning. Loose "feels like AGI" definitions would push the number up.
- **"via LLMs"**: Modern frontier systems are already hybrids (transformer core + RL + tool use + retrieval + agentic scaffolding + multimodal encoders). I count "transformer/next-token core plus heavy augmentation" as *LLM path*. I count things like LeCun-style JEPA/world-model-first systems or genuine neuro-symbolic architectures as *fundamentally different*. The borderline cases are where reasonable people disagree most.

## Decomposition

**P(AGI by 2035) ≈ 0.35**

Factors pushing up: extraordinary capability gains, $100B+ capital flows, reasoning-model progress, credible insiders (Hassabis, Amodei) predicting 2027–2032.

Factors pushing down: strict definition, the field's long history of overconfident predictions, lab incentives to hype, and unsolved bottlenecks that may need new ideas—continual/online learning, reliability/hallucination, robust long-horizon reasoning, sample efficiency, physical grounding. Benchmark saturation ≠ real-world generality.

**P(via LLMs | AGI by 2035) ≈ 0.65**

The strongest argument *for* a high conditional: short timelines favor the incumbent paradigm. A fundamentally new architecture would need to be invented, scaled, *and* proven within ~10 years—hard. So if AGI is near, it's probably an extension of what we have.

What pulls it back down: many serious researchers (not just Marcus) think pure scaling plateaus and that the missing ingredients are substantial enough that the resulting system might fairly be called "fundamentally different."

**Combined: 0.35 × 0.65 ≈ 0.23**

## Consistency check (the full outcome space by 2035)

- No AGI: ~0.65
- AGI via LLM lineage: ~0.23
- AGI via fundamentally different architecture: ~0.12

(Sums to 1.0. Note the third bucket is smaller precisely because short timelines disadvantage new paradigms.)

## On the Marcus quote

My estimate implicitly says Marcus is *probably* directionally right that pure LLMs aren't sufficient for full AGI—but that the most likely successful path still *runs through* LLMs as a core component. His "game over" framing overclaims; a single benchmark or model result rarely settles a paradigm question. But betting heavily that scaled LLMs *deliver* full AGI within a decade also overclaims.

**Bottom line: 0.22**, with genuine uncertainty spanning roughly 0.15–0.30 depending mostly on how you draw the "LLM vs. fundamentally different" line.

- **Bull factors:** Genuinely fast 2022–2025 progress (reasoning models, agents, tool use); METR-style data showing the length of tasks AI can complete doubling roughly every ~7 months; unprecedented capital and talent; lab leaders forecasting 2–10 years.
- **Bear factors:** The last mile is hard — reliability, robustness, out-of-distribution generalization, and continual learning remain unsolved; benchmark gains overstate real-world generality; possible diminishing returns to scaling; AI's long history of overpromising; real economic deployment lagging hype.

Serious forecasters span ~10% to ~50% here. I land near 30%.

## 2. P(it's LLM-lineage | AGI by 2035) ≈ 80%

This is where the **definitional crux** lives, and where I'd push back hardest on Marcus's certainty.

If AGI arrives within 10 years, it almost has to be a *descendant of today's paradigm* — large pretrained neural nets, transformer-ish cores — because that's where all the resources, talent, and momentum are concentrated, and a decade is too short for a from-scratch paradigm (symbolic AI, brain emulation, etc.) to mature and overtake from behind.

**But** a 2033 AGI will likely bolt on heavy RL, search, memory, world models, multimodality, and continual-learning mechanisms. Whether you call that "still an LLM" or "fundamentally different" is partly semantic. Marcus would say the necessary additions *are* the fundamental change; scaling proponents would say it's all one continuous lineage. I read his "fundamentally different architectures" as meaning a clean break from the deep-learning-scaling paradigm — and conditional on fast AGI, that break is unlikely to be what gets there first.

Important: there's **positive correlation** between the two factors. Worlds where AGI comes fast are disproportionately worlds where scaling "just works," which pushes this conditional *up*. Conversely, if AGI needs genuinely new ideas, it's both *less likely by 2035* and *less likely to be LLM-based* — both factors move together.

## Combining

0.30 × 0.80 ≈ **0.24**

Adjusting for definitional fuzziness, I'll call it **~22%**.

"Game over" is overconfident in one direction, just as "AGI by 2027" is overconfident in the other. My estimate implies:

## It's two questions, not one

So the scenario "fundamentally different architecture delivers AGI *by 2035*" is somewhat self-limiting: the more you believe LLMs are a dead end, the *less* likely AGI-by-any-means happens this decade. That's part of why the bulk of near-term AGI probability lands in the LLM bucket.

rows.answer:
Short answer up front: **~30%**, but that number hides most of the interesting content, so let me decompose it.

The headline probability is really a product of two separate uncertainties:

**1. Will AGI exist at all by 2035?**
**2. Conditional on that, will it come from the LLM lineage rather than a fundamentally new paradigm?**

## Component 1: P(AGI by 2035) ≈ 40%

Using a demanding bar (matches or exceeds human experts across essentially all cognitive domains, with robust reasoning, learning, and autonomy):

- **Pushing it up:** the scaling track record has been remarkably reliable; reasoning/test-time-compute models opened a genuinely new improvement axis; capital deployment is unprecedented (hundreds of billions in compute); benchmarks keep saturating faster than predicted.
- **Pushing it down:** signs of diminishing returns to pretraining, high-quality data limits, persistent reliability/hallucination/robustness gaps, weak continual learning and long-horizon agency, lagging physical-world competence, and a long history of AI overpromising. Broad researcher surveys (e.g., Grace et al. 2023) still imply only ~25–35% by 2035.

I land around 40%, slightly above the survey median but below lab-leader optimism. Defensible range: 25–50%.

## Component 2: P(LLM-lineage | AGI by 2035) ≈ 75%

The key insight here is a **selection effect on the timeline**: fundamentally new architectures take a long time to invent, scale, and mature (deep learning itself simmered from the 1980s to ~2012). A genuinely new paradigm would have to be invented *and* overtake a massively-resourced incumbent within ~10 years. So worlds where AGI arrives *by 2035* are disproportionately worlds where the current paradigm sufficed. If a new paradigm is actually required, that mostly pushes the date past 2035 (showing up as a failure in Component 1, not as a non-LLM win in Component 2).

The counterweight: approaches like LeCun's JEPA/world models, energy-based models, or neuro-symbolic systems could supply a missing ingredient and might reasonably be called "fundamentally different." Defensible range: 60–85%.

**Combined: 0.40 × 0.75 ≈ 0.30.**

## The crucial caveat: "via LLMs" is doing enormous work

This is where the Marcus-vs-scalers debate gets semantic. Frontier systems already aren't pure next-token predictors—they're multimodal, RL-tuned, tool-using, and agentic. My ~30% assumes the **generous interpretation**: anything in the transformer-foundation-model lineage counts as an "LLM," even with heavy additions like RL on reasoning and agent scaffolding.

Under a **strict interpretation**—"pure scaling of language modeling, no fundamentally new ingredient"—I'd drop to ~10–15%, because I think additional ingredients beyond raw scaling are likely needed (and are arguably already being bolted on). Marcus can claim partial vindication or partial defeat depending entirely on which definition you adopt.

## My single number

**~30%** (generous interpretation, which I think matches the spirit of the question).

Note what this implies about the Marcus quote: I'm *not* endorsing "game over." But my estimate does mean I think it's more likely than not that, by 2035, *either* AGI hasn't arrived *or* it arrived through something Marcus could plausibly call "not just an LLM." His confident framing ("game over") is overclaiming on present evidence—but so is the assumption that scaling alone clearly finishes the job.

Short answer up front: **~25–30% (point estimate ~0.27)** for the literal reading, with a big asterisk on definitions. Here's the reasoning.

## Why this needs decomposing

The question bundles two separate events, and the answer hinges heavily on definitions:

1. Will AGI (demanding sense: matches expert humans across most cognitive/economic tasks, robustly) arrive **at all** by 2035?
2. **If** it does, will the winning system be "LLM-based" or "fundamentally different"?

There's also a definitional landmine: today's frontier systems already aren't pure LLMs. They fold in RL, tool use, chain-of-thought/inference-time search, multimodality, retrieval, and agentic scaffolding. "LLM vs. fundamentally different" is a continuum, not a binary — and much of the Marcus-style debate is partly semantic. I'll treat **"via LLMs"** as "a system whose central engine is a large self-supervised foundation model, however heavily augmented," and **"fundamentally different"** as one whose core learning paradigm is qualitatively non-LLM (primarily symbolic, world-model-first à la LeCun's JEPA, brain-like, or something not yet invented).

## Factor 1 — P(AGI by 2035) ≈ 35–40%

- **For:** stunning 2020–2025 progress; new scaling axes (RL + inference-time compute) reopened gains just as pretraining returns looked to flatten; enormous capital/talent/compute; many insiders forecast <10 years.
- **Against:** persistent reliability/hallucination/out-of-distribution/continual-learning weaknesses; AI's long history of being "20 years away"; data ceilings; the "last mile" to robust generality is historically brutal; deployment friction means even capable systems take time to *count* as AGI.

## Factor 2 — P(LLM-based | AGI by 2035) ≈ 0.70

The dominant driver is **incumbent advantage over a short horizon.** Nearly all momentum, money, and compute sit in the foundation-model paradigm. For a "fundamentally different" architecture to win by 2035, it would have to be invented, matured, scaled, *and* overtake LLMs in ~10 years — hard for a challenger. The shorter the path to AGI, the more likely it's an evolution of today's systems. Counterweight: credible researchers (LeCun et al.) think autoregressive LLMs are a dead end, and if the necessary new ingredients count as "fundamentally different," this number drops.

## Combining

- **Joint event (literal reading):** 0.38 × 0.70 ≈ **0.27** (~1 in 4). Defensible range ~0.15–0.40.
- **If you meant the conditional** ("given AGI happens, LLM vs. other?"): ~**0.70**.

## My single number: **~0.27 (≈25–30%)**

## On the Marcus post

"LLMs aren't the royal road" and "AGI will be LLM-based" can *both* be partly true. The likeliest 2035 outcome isn't "pure scaled-up GPT" (Marcus is probably right that scaling alone won't get there) but a **hybrid with a foundation model at its core plus substantial additional machinery** — which most people would still file under "LLM-based," even though it's nothing like what "LLM" meant in 2020. So a lot of who "wins" this argument will come down to where you draw the definitional line, not just what gets built.

rows.answer:
**Short answer: ~25%.**

Here's how I get there, because the number is meaningless without unpacking two cruxes that dominate it.

## Crux 1: What counts as "AGI"?

I'll use a moderately demanding definition: a system that can perform essentially all economically valuable cognitive work a competent human professional can, across domains, *robustly* (not just on benchmarks), including learning genuinely new tasks reasonably efficiently. A weaker "transformative but jagged" definition would push my number up; a stronger "matches humans at literally everything including efficient continual learning and embodiment" definition would push it down.

## Crux 2: What counts as "via LLMs"?

This is where the Marcus framing is a bit of a false binary. Frontier systems are already not pure next-token predictors — they're transformers plus RLHF, RL on verifiable rewards (reasoning models), tool use, retrieval, multimodality, and agentic scaffolding. I'll treat "via LLMs" broadly as *the current paradigm and its direct descendants* (large-scale pretraining on transformers, augmented and scaled), versus "fundamentally different" meaning a genuinely new core (e.g., a non-transformer world-model architecture à la LeCun's JEPA, neurosymbolic-from-scratch, or something not yet invented becoming the *central* engine).

## The decomposition

**P(AGI by 2035)** under the above definition: I put this around **~33%** (defensible range 15–45%).
- *Up:* unprecedented capital, scaling laws that keep finding new axes (inference-time compute, RL), repeated absorption of skeptics' "walls," and capability trends like steadily lengthening autonomous task horizons.
- *Down:* genuine unsolved problems (reliability over long horizons, continual learning, out-of-distribution robustness, hard-to-verify domains where RL signals are weak), data limits, and only a 10-year window — strong AGI in a decade is historically a tall order.

**P(it's the LLM paradigm | AGI by 2035): ~0.75–0.80.**
The key logic: *short timelines strongly favor the incumbent.* A truly new architecture would have to mature, scale, and overtake a paradigm with a ~8-year head start, all the infrastructure, tooling, talent, and hundreds of billions in capital — within 10 years. If AGI comes *soon*, it almost has to be built mostly on what we already have. (The further out AGI is, the more room a new paradigm has to win.)

**Combined:** 0.33 × 0.77 ≈ **0.25**.

## What would move this number

- **Toward 35–40%:** weaker AGI definition, or continued smooth capability scaling in messy/unverifiable domains.
- **Toward 10–15%:** evidence that current systems have a hard ceiling on robust reasoning/agency that scaling + RL doesn't fix, *and* a credible alternative emerges.

## On the Marcus framing

"Game over" is overconfident in the same way "AGI by 2027, guaranteed" is. Marcus has real points — hallucination, brittle reasoning, the inadequacy of *pure* scaling. But he's declared walls before that the paradigm climbed over by adding new techniques rather than new architectures, and his critique often targets a 2020-vintage "just scale next-token prediction" strawman rather than the actual moving target. The honest state is wide uncertainty, not a verdict.

My single number: **~25%**, with most of the uncertainty living in your chosen definition of "AGI."

- The GPT-3 → GPT-4 → reasoning-model (o1/o3) trajectory has been faster than almost anyone predicted, and o3-class results on ARC-AGI and frontier math surprised even skeptics.
- ~$100B+/year flowing into compute, and AI is starting to accelerate AI research itself.
- Lab insiders with the most information (Amodei, Hassabis, Altman) are bullish on a 3–7 year horizon.

Arguments pulling down:
- "AGI" is a stringent bar—robust reasoning, long-horizon agency, continual learning, reliability—and benchmark wins keep outrunning real-world economic generality.
- AI has a decades-long track record of "5 years away" overoptimism.
- Plausible walls: data exhaustion, diminishing pretraining returns (the reason labs pivoted to inference-time compute), energy/chips, persistent hallucination and brittleness.

**P(it's the LLM lineage | AGI happens by 2035) ≈ 70–80%**

This is the part people underweight. *If* AGI arrives within ~10 years, that's itself strong evidence the current paradigm got there—because a genuinely new architecture would need to be invented, scaled, *and* matured fast enough to overtake a paradigm with an enormous head start in talent, capital, and tooling. New paradigms (LeCun's JEPA, neurosymbolic, brain-inspired) are mostly nascent. Short timelines favor the incumbent.

Multiplying: ~0.42 × ~0.75 ≈ **0.31–0.33**.

## The biggest source of disagreement is definitional

What counts as "via LLMs" swings this a lot:
- **Broad reading** (transformer/self-supervised foundation models, *plus* RL, multimodality, inference-time search, tools, memory, agentic scaffolding): the conditional jumps toward 0.85, and the whole estimate rises to ~38%.
- **Strict reading** (basically GPT-style scaling with no fundamentally new components): the conditional drops toward 0.5–0.6, because new modules for world-modeling or reasoning may be needed—and you'd then have to argue about whether a hybrid still "counts."

Note the irony: o1/o3-style RL-on-reasoning already counters the strict "LLMs can't reason" critique, but it's *also* the kind of bolt-on that blurs the line Marcus is drawing.

## What would move my estimate

- **Up:** another capability jump from the reasoning-model paradigm; clear evidence of reliable long-horizon agency; AI meaningfully compressing its own R&D cycle.
- **Down:** a visible plateau in the 2026–2028 model generation; continued failure on continual learning and real-world reliability despite scaling; a non-LLM approach showing surprising generality.

## On Marcus specifically

His narrow claim—"pure scaling of next-token prediction plateaus"—has *partial* support (it's why the field pivoted to test-time compute). His broad claim—"LLMs are not the road to AGI at all"—is a confident bet against the only paradigm with momentum, and I'd put well under even odds on it being vindicated by 2035. "Game over" is rhetoric, not a forecast.

My honest meta-point: the dominant uncertainty is whether AGI happens at all this decade, not which paradigm wins if it does. Anyone quoting you a number with two significant figures (including me) is dressing up deep uncertainty.

rows.answer:
**My single-number estimate: ~30%**

The cleanest way to get there is to decompose the question into two parts, because it's really a joint probability.

## Part 1: Will AGI be achieved at all by 2035?

My estimate: **~40%** (with a wide honest range of 25–55%)

Factors pushing higher:
- Extraordinary pace of progress 2020–2025, plus continued compute/capital scaling
- The reasoning-model paradigm (o1/o3-style inference-time compute, RL on verifiable rewards) reopened a scaling axis just as pretraining returns looked shaky
- Rapidly improving agentic/tool-use capabilities
- Recent track record has favored the optimists on *near-term* milestones

Factors pushing lower:
- Genuinely unsolved problems that scaling hasn't obviously cracked: continual/online learning, reliable long-horizon agency, robust reasoning without confabulation, sample efficiency, grounding
- Signs of diminishing returns on pure pretraining
- "AGI" is a demanding bar, and the field has a long history of premature "almost there" moments
- Data, energy, and hardware constraints

## Part 2: *If* AGI arrives by 2035, is it LLM-descended rather than a fundamentally new architecture?

My estimate: **~70%** conditional

The key logic: **short timelines favor the incumbent paradigm.** A fundamentally different architecture (LeCun-style JEPA world models, neurosymbolic systems, something not-yet-invented) would need to be developed, validated, *and* scaled within ~10 years against the enormous momentum, infrastructure, talent, and capital locked into transformers. That's a tall order. The faster AGI comes, the more it almost has to be "more of the current thing."

The main caveat working the other direction: the boundary is blurry. Frontier systems are *already* hybrids (transformers + RLHF + search + tool use + multimodality + memory). If the missing ingredient turns out to be a world-model core that doesn't run on next-token prediction, reasonable people would call that "fundamentally different" even if it borrows transformer components.

## Combining

0.40 × 0.70 ≈ **0.28, round to ~30%**

## On the Marcus framing

Note that my ~70% "no" is mostly *not* a vindication of Marcus. The bulk of that probability mass is "AGI just hasn't arrived by 2035," which says nothing about whether LLMs were a dead end—only that they hadn't finished the job. The slice that actually matches his thesis (AGI *does* arrive, but via something architecturally new) is fairly small in my model. His "game over" confidence seems unwarranted in *either* direction: he's overconfident that LLMs fail, while LLM maximalists are overconfident they suffice.

**Biggest source of error in this estimate is definitional** — what counts as "AGI" and what counts as "still an LLM" can each swing the number by 15+ points. If you want, tell me which definitions you have in mind and I'll re-estimate.

Today's frontier systems are already not pure language models—they include RL-based reasoning (o1/o3-style), tool use, retrieval, multimodal training, and agentic scaffolding. I'll treat "via LLMs" as *the current transformer + large-scale pretraining + post-training lineage and its incremental descendants*, versus "fundamentally different" meaning a genuinely new paradigm (e.g., dominant neurosymbolic systems, brain-inspired architectures, or a new learning framework). - *Downward pressure:* persistent brittleness in reasoning and out-of-distribution generalization, no robust continual learning, data limits, some evidence of diminishing returns on pure pretraining, a long history of AI overoptimism, and "AGI" being a stringent, moving target.

rows.answer:
Short answer: roughly **20%** (with a defensible range of ~10–30%).

Here's how I get there. This is really a joint probability of two things, so I'll decompose it.

## Factor 1: Will AGI be achieved at all by 2035?

This depends heavily on definition, which is the biggest source of disagreement. Using a moderate-but-real bar (matches or exceeds human experts across most cognitive/economically-valuable tasks, with reasonable robustness):

**Reasons it might happen (~higher):**
- Compute and capital are still scaling hard, and a new axis (inference-time/reasoning compute) is currently delivering gains beyond pure pretraining.
- Algorithmic progress is compounding; "it won't work" predictions have a poor track record this decade.

**Reasons it might not (~lower):**
- Pretraining data limits; reasoning models may have their own ceilings.
- The hard part of AGI—reliability, continual learning, robust world models, low hallucination—is exactly where current systems are weakest. The "last 10%" tends to take the longest (cf. self-driving: fast initial capability, then a brutal reliability tail).

I'd put **P(AGI by 2035) ≈ 30%**, with wide error bars.

## Factor 2: Given AGI by 2035, is it via LLMs?

Here's the counterintuitive part: a *short* timeline actually favors the LLM lineage. A fundamentally new architecture would need to be invented, scaled, and matured in under a decade, competing against the enormous existing investment, tooling, and talent in transformers. New paradigms (JEPA/world models, neurosymbolic, active inference) are mostly still pre-scaling.

So **conditional on AGI by 2035, I'd estimate ~65–75% that it's LLM-lineage.** Call it 70%.

Big caveat: the LLM-vs.-"fundamentally different" line is blurry. Frontier systems are already multimodal, RL-trained, tool-using, agentic, and search-augmented—not "pure" LLMs. If the winning system is transformer-descended + a lot of new machinery, reasonable people will dispute the label. I'm counting transformer-descended, language-pretrained, scaled systems as "via LLMs."

## Combining

P ≈ 0.30 × 0.70 ≈ **0.21**

A coherent way to see the whole space:
- No AGI by 2035: ~70%
- AGI via LLM-lineage: ~20%
- AGI via fundamentally different architecture: ~10%

## On the Marcus framing

His critiques of LLM brittleness, hallucination, and weak reasoning are substantively real, and I wouldn't dismiss them. But "Game over" is rhetorical, and his track record also includes underestimating how far scaling-plus-augmentation would go. The honest position isn't "he's right" or "he's wrong"—it's genuine uncertainty. Notably, even if you think LLMs *alone* won't reach AGI, the practical path likely runs *through* LLM-lineage systems with added components, which makes the binary framing somewhat artificial.

## What would move my number

- **Up:** continued gains from reasoning/agentic systems on long-horizon, reliability-sensitive tasks; clear progress on continual learning.
- **Down:** a visible plateau in frontier capability; or, conversely, a striking demonstration from a non-transformer paradigm (which would shift probability from the LLM bucket to the "different architecture" bucket without necessarily changing the total AGI-by-2035 odds).

Treat the **~20%** as a soft central estimate—the definitional choices on both "AGI" and "LLM" can swing it by 10+ points either way.

I'll settle on around 22% as my estimate, though I should note that both "AGI" and "LLM" are slippery definitions that heavily influence this number. I'm also reconsidering whether 30% for P(AGI by 2035) might be slightly conservative given the pace of progress and resources being deployed, though "AGI" in a truly robust, general sense is still a high bar.

On Gary Marcus's "game over" claim: he's been consistently skeptical of LLMs and has been right about some limitations like hallucination and reasoning brittleness, but his declaration seems premature. rows.answer:
Short answer up front: **~20%** (my single best estimate), with most of my plausible range falling between 15% and 30%.

Here's how I get there, because the number depends heavily on decomposing two separate questions.

## The decomposition

The claim "AGI via LLMs by 2035" is really a conjunction:

**P(AGI via LLMs by 2035) = P(AGI achieved at all by 2035) × P(it's LLM-lineage | AGI by 2035)**

These pull in opposite directions, which is the interesting part.

## Factor 1: Will AGI arrive at all by 2035?

My estimate: **~30%** (for a demanding definition: broadly human-level across most cognitive/economic tasks, robust and reliable).

- *For:* Extraordinary pace 2020–2025, enormous capital/compute/talent inflow, and a new scaling vector (test-time compute / RL-on-reasoning) reviving momentum just as pretraining returns flattened.
- *Against:* Unsolved hard problems—reliability, long-horizon agentic coherence, continual learning, genuine out-of-distribution reasoning. These are exactly the limitations skeptics flagged years ago, and they haven't fully fallen. "By 2035" sounds far, but a strong AGI definition is a high bar.
- Expert surveys (e.g., AI Impacts) historically put the *median* for high-level machine intelligence in the 2040s–2060s, though that's been shifting earlier. Lab leaders say 2026–2030 but are self-interested.

## Factor 2: If AGI arrives by 2035, is it LLM-lineage?

My estimate: **~70%**.

Here's the counterintuitive bit: a *short* timeline actually **favors** the LLM paradigm. Fundamentally new architectures (LeCun-style world models/JEPA, neurosymbolic, brain-inspired approaches) typically need many years to go from research to scaled dominance. If AGI shows up within ~10 years, it most likely grows out of the paradigm that already has the infrastructure, momentum, and head start—even if heavily augmented with tools, agents, RL, retrieval, and multimodal training.

The catch is definitional: by 2035 the systems may be so hybridized that "is this still an LLM?" becomes genuinely ambiguous. I'm interpreting "via LLMs" generously as "the current transformer/self-supervised-pretraining lineage plus augmentations," vs. Marcus's implied "something fundamentally new."

## Combining

0.30 × 0.70 ≈ **0.21**, which I'll round to **~20%**.

## On Marcus specifically

His "game over" is rhetorically overconfident—LLMs have repeatedly humbled their skeptics. But he's *partly* vindicated: the field's pivot to test-time compute and RL is itself an implicit admission that naive pretraining scaling was hitting limits. The honest synthesis is that "pure LLMs scaled forever" probably isn't the road to AGI, but "current paradigm + heavy augmentation" still has real runway. Both his triumphalist critics and his "game over" framing are overstating their cases.

**Bottom line: ~20%**, driven mostly by genuine uncertainty about whether *any* path reaches AGI by 2035, not by doubt that LLMs would be the vehicle if it happens that soon.

rows.answer:
**Short answer: ~25%.**

This proposition actually bundles two questions, and it's worth separating them:

**1. Will AGI be achieved at all by 2035?**

This is the bigger source of uncertainty. My estimate: ~35–40%. Reasoning:
- *Pushing higher:* Rapid recent progress, a new scaling axis (inference-time/"reasoning" compute), massive capital, and the possibility of AI accelerating AI research.
- *Pushing lower:* "AGI" is a high bar (robust performance across essentially all cognitive tasks, with autonomy and reliability). Persistent problems—hallucination, brittle long-horizon reasoning, weak continual learning, the gap between benchmark scores and real-world reliability—remain unsolved. Data constraints and possible diminishing returns to scaling. The field also has a long history of overestimating nearness.

**2. *If* AGI arrives by 2035, will it be "via LLMs" rather than something fundamentally different?**

Conditional estimate: ~65–75%. Key point: a *short* timeline actually favors the LLM lineage. Genuinely novel paradigms (neurosymbolic systems, explicit world models, etc.) historically take decades to mature from inception to dominance. So the worlds where AGI shows up within ~10 years are disproportionately the worlds where the current paradigm—scaled, multimodal, RL-tuned, agentic, tool-using transformers—turned out to be sufficient. Marcus's scenario (LLMs are a dead end, something fundamentally different is required) is *more* likely in the longer-timeline worlds, which mostly fall outside 2035.

**Combining:** ~0.38 × ~0.70 ≈ **0.25–0.27**.

**The crucial caveat:** this number is extremely sensitive to definitions, and the "LLM vs. fundamentally different" framing is somewhat artificial. The likely path to AGI is *hybrid and evolutionary*—LLMs as the substrate plus a lot of additional machinery (search, memory, RL, possibly symbolic or world-model components). Whether you call that "an LLM" or "fundamentally different" is partly a labeling choice. Under a broad reading of "LLM" (any large transformer-based foundation model and its descendants), the figure rises toward 30%; under a strict reading (pure next-token prediction with no major new ingredients), it falls below 15%.

On Marcus specifically: he's been *directionally* skeptical for a long time and the field has repeatedly surprised skeptics on capabilities—but his core critiques about reliability and reasoning have also repeatedly proven non-trivial. "Game over" is overconfident in one direction; "AGI within a few years, definitely via LLMs" is overconfident in the other.

**My single best number: ~25%.**

rows.answer:
The honest answer hinges on a definitional crux, so let me separate that out first, then give you a number.

**The crux: what counts as "via LLMs"?**

Today's frontier systems are already not pure LLMs—they're transformers trained on next-token prediction *plus* RLHF/RLVR, tool use, chain-of-thought/test-time compute, multimodality, and agentic scaffolding. So "LLM" is a moving target. The reasonable interpretation of your question (especially given Marcus's framing, where he argues you need something Marcus-style *different*, like neurosymbolic systems) is:

- **"Via LLMs"** = the path that's recognizably descended from and centrally reliant on the large-scale pretrained transformer paradigm, even with lots of bolt-ons.
- **"Fundamentally different"** = the core intelligence comes from a genuinely new mechanism (JEPA/world-model approaches à la LeCun, heavy neurosymbolic, brain-inspired, or something not yet invented).

I'll use that generous reading of "LLM."

**Decomposition:**

I think of this as a joint probability of two events:

1. **P(AGI of some meaningful kind by 2035) ≈ 0.35–0.45.** Progress 2020–2025 was fast, and labs/capital/talent all point the same direction—but the hard parts of AGI (robust long-horizon autonomy, continual learning, reliability, genuine novel reasoning) remain unsolved and could easily eat the decade. Expert surveys and superforecasters skew later than lab leaders. Call it **~0.40**.

2. **P(it's LLM-lineage | AGI by 2035) ≈ 0.70–0.80.** This is the key insight: a *short* timeline is itself evidence *for* the incumbent paradigm. Ten years is barely enough to invent a fundamentally new architecture, scale it, *and* have it overtake a paradigm with hundreds of billions in infrastructure behind it. The two beliefs are correlated—if LLMs hit a hard wall (Marcus's view), AGI-by-2035 itself becomes much less likely, because a replacement would need time to mature. Call it **~0.75**.

**Joint estimate: ~0.30.**

**My single number: ~30%** that AGI is achieved via the (broadly construed) LLM paradigm by 2035.

If you instead meant the *conditional* ("given AGI arrives by 2035, is it LLM-based?"), my answer is **~75%**.

**Key sensitivities:**
- If you define "LLM" *strictly* (core must be autoregressive token prediction, no major new mechanism), drop the conditional to ~0.55–0.65, and the joint to ~0.25.
- The likeliest real-world outcome may make the question semantic: Marcus is partly right that pure scaling plateaus, *and* the field routes around it with LLM-centric hybrids—which you could file under either label.

So: **~30%** as my best single number, with genuine uncertainty roughly spanning 0.15–0.40 depending on definitions.

rows.answer:
**Headline estimate: ~20%** — that we reach AGI by 2035 *and* it comes primarily from the LLM lineage.

That number is a product of two judgments, and it's worth separating them because they answer different questions:

| Component | My estimate |
|---|---|
| P(robust, broadly human-level AGI by 2035 at all) | ~30% |
| P(it's LLM-descended *given* AGI arrives by 2035) | ~70–80% |
| **Joint (the question as literally asked)** | **~20%** |

So if you actually mean the *conditional* question — "if AGI shows up by 2035, will it be LLM-based rather than a new paradigm?" — my answer is **~75%**. That's the more decision-relevant number for the Marcus debate, since he's making a claim about the *paradigm*, not just the clock.

**Why the conditional is high (LLMs favored if AGI comes soon):**
- **Path dependence.** Nearly all capital, compute, and talent is pointed at transformers + large-scale pretraining + RL. A "fundamentally different" architecture would have to be invented, scaled, *and* matured within the window while overtaking a massively-funded incumbent. Short timelines favor whoever already has a head start.
- **The frontier is already drifting** from "pure LLM" toward multimodal, tool-using, RL-trained, inference-time-reasoning systems — but these are still LLM-*descended*. New ingredients are mostly being bolted onto the transformer core, not replacing it.
- Counterpoint: transformers went from invention (2017) to GPT-4 (2023) in ~6 years, so a fast paradigm shift isn't impossible. That's why I don't put the conditional above ~80%.

**Why P(AGI by 2035) is well under 50%:**
- Genuine unsolved problems — reliable reasoning, long-horizon autonomy, continual learning, robust world models, hallucination/reliability. Marcus is *directionally right* that scale alone hasn't dissolved these.
- Signs of diminishing returns on pure pretraining, data limits, and a persistent gap between benchmark scores and real-world robustness.
- Base rate: AGI has been "~20 years away" for decades; near-term overoptimism is the historical norm.

**The hidden crux — it's mostly a definitions problem:**
- "AGI" ranges from *"median knowledge worker across most tasks"* (plausibly 40%+ by 2035) to *"matches or exceeds all human experts, including novel science"* (maybe 10–15%). Pick your bar and the number swings massively.
- "LLM vs. fundamentally different" is a semi-false binary. The likely winner is a **hybrid** that keeps large-scale self-supervised transformers at its core but adds memory, search, world models, and maybe neuro-symbolic components. Whether you file that under "LLMs, evolved" or "something new" is partly a labeling choice — and that choice alone moves the estimate by 20+ points.

**On Marcus specifically:** "scaling alone isn't sufficient" is defensible and increasingly mainstream. "Game over / LLMs are not the royal road" likely overshoots, because the field is absorbing exactly the ingredients he says are missing *while keeping LLMs central*. The paradigm may get there even if naive scaling doesn't.

**Important context:** the single most likely 2035 outcome is still *"no AGI yet"* (~70%). So my ~20% is fully compatible with Marcus being right that it's "not imminent" while being overconfident on the architecture claim.

Caveat: these are calibrated subjective judgments, not outputs of a model — reasonable experts span roughly 10–40% on the joint question, driven almost entirely by how strictly they define "AGI."

rows.answer:
**Short answer: ~33%** (my plausible range is roughly 25–45% depending heavily on definitions).

Here's how I get there. The question is really a conjunction of two things, so I'll decompose it.

## Factor 1: P(AGI by 2035 at all) ≈ 40%

Key considerations:
- **For:** Enormous capital/talent inflows, continued rapid progress (reasoning models, agents, tool use) that has repeatedly outpaced skeptic predictions of imminent plateaus, lab leaders forecasting AGI within 5–10 years.
- **Against:** "AGI" is a slippery target. A *robust* version (reliable, autonomous, continual learning, genuine reasoning, not just benchmark-saturating) is much harder than demos suggest. The field has a long history of 10-year predictions that didn't land. Real unsolved problems: reliability, hallucination, long-horizon planning, sample efficiency, possible data/compute/energy walls.
- The number swings a lot with definition: a loose "transformative, broadly capable" bar → 50%+; a strict "matches humans on essentially all cognitive work including autonomous learning" bar → 30% or less.

## Factor 2: P(it's via LLMs | AGI by 2035) ≈ 78%

rows.answer:
**Short answer: ~25%** (joint probability that, by 2035, we have AGI *and* it was reached primarily via LLM-descended systems).

Here's how I get there.

## The key definitional problem

"LLM" vs. "fundamentally different architecture" is a fuzzy boundary, and your answer depends heavily on where you draw it. Today's frontier systems are already not pure next-token predictors—they include RL, chain-of-thought reasoning, tool use, multimodality, and agentic scaffolding. If AGI arrives as an *evolution* of this paradigm (transformer core + lots of additions), is that "via LLMs" or "something new"? I'll treat **"LLM-descended"** generously—if a large transformer trained on broad data is a core component, it counts as "via LLMs."

## Decomposition

I split this into two questions:

**1. Will AGI exist at all by 2035? (~35%)**
- Bull case: 2022–2025 progress has been startling; reasoning models and agents are closing gaps fast; lab leaders forecast 5–10 years.
- Bear case: pure pretraining scaling shows clear diminishing returns (which is *why* the field pivoted to inference-time compute), and hard problems remain—reliability, continual learning, robust out-of-distribution reasoning, grounded world models.
- Aggregating expert views and adjusting for "AGI" being a demanding, moving target, I land around **30–40%**.

**2. Conditional on AGI by 2035, is it LLM-descended? (~70%)**
- The strongest argument here is **timeline mechanics**. Going from a novel architecture to a mature, scaled, AGI-grade system historically takes ~5–10 years (transformer: 2017 → ChatGPT: 2022 → frontier reasoning: 2025). If a *genuinely new* paradigm were required and hasn't clearly emerged yet, it's hard to invent, scale, and mature it before 2035.
- So if AGI comes this soon, it most likely rides on the current paradigm's descendants. Counterweight: the additions needed (memory, planning, world models) might come to dominate enough that people call the result "not an LLM."

**Combined: 0.35 × 0.70 ≈ 0.25**

## What this implies about the Marcus claim

Notably, most of the probability mass where "Marcus is right" lives in the world where **AGI simply doesn't arrive by 2035** (~65%)—which vindicates a *timeline* skepticism more than his specific *architectural* claim. The world where AGI arrives by 2035 *but* via a non-LLM paradigm is relatively small (~10%), precisely because new architectures take a long time to mature.

## My single number: ~25%

With honest error bars I'd accept anything from **15% to 35%**. The dominant uncertainty is the AGI-timeline question, not the architecture question—conditional on near-term AGI, LLM-descended systems are the favorite by default.

One caveat worth flagging: a confident *low* number here (like Marcus's "game over") requires being confident about both the timeline *and* the architecture, and being that certain about either—let alone both—isn't well-supported given how fast and unpredictably the field has moved.

## Untangling the question

What you're really asking is a *joint* probability:

**P(AGI by 2035) × P(it's LLM-based | AGI by 2035)**

Two definitional problems make any number inherently squishy:

- **"AGI"** has no agreed definition. It ranges from "beats humans at most economically valuable cognitive work" (achievable-ish) to "matches human general intelligence across virtually all domains, including efficient learning and embodiment" (much harder).
- **"LLM-based vs. fundamentally different" is a spectrum, not a binary.** Frontier systems already aren't pure LLMs — they bolt on RLHF, multimodality, tool use, retrieval, chain-of-thought reasoning, and agentic scaffolding. I'll count something as "LLM-based" if it's recognizably descended from the transformer/foundation-model lineage, even with major new components.

## Factor 1: Does AGI arrive by 2035 at all?

| For | Against |
|---|---|
| Continued scaling gains | Possible diminishing returns to scale |
| Reasoning/test-time compute as a fresh axis | High-quality data limits |
| Rapid agentic progress | Persistent unreliability, hallucination |
| Enormous capital & infrastructure | Weak continual learning, fragile world-models |
| | Energy/compute/chip constraints |
| | AI's long history of overpromising |

I put this around **30–35%** for a reasonably demanding definition — lower for stringent ones, higher for loose economic ones.

## Factor 2: If AGI arrives by 2035, is it LLM-based?

Short timelines *favor the incumbent paradigm*. A fundamentally new architecture would have to emerge, mature, and scale to AGI within ~10 years while LLMs hold a massive lead in investment, talent, and infrastructure. New paradigms also tend to absorb rather than replace successful predecessors. So conditional on *early* AGI, I'd put roughly **70%** on it being transformer/LLM-descended (likely hybridized).

## Putting it together

~0.33 × ~0.70 ≈ **0.23**

## On Marcus specifically

He's been directionally right that pure scaling hits walls and that LLMs have genuine reasoning/reliability gaps. But "game over" is rhetoric, not analysis — and his track record includes repeated premature declarations that the current approach has peaked. The honest stance is deep uncertainty, not confidence in either direction. (Note also that lab leaders like Amodei/Hassabis price this far higher than I do, and they have inside information *and* strong incentives to be bullish — so I'm landing between the optimists and the skeptics.)

## My single-number estimate: **~25%**

The reason it's not higher: the *modal* outcome for 2035 is probably **"no full AGI, but extremely capable LLM-descended systems"** — with smaller probability mass on both "a fundamentally new architecture wins" and "LLMs go all the way." The number swings most on how you define AGI: tighten the definition and I'd drop toward ~12%; loosen it to "automates most economically valuable cognitive work" and I'd rise toward ~35%.

Short answer up front: **roughly 28%** (call it "high-20s to low-30s percent"). Here's how I get there and why the number is so sensitive to definitions.

## The question is really two questions multiplied together

P(AGI via LLMs by 2035) = **P(AGI of any kind by 2035)** × **P(it's LLM-lineage | AGI happens)**

And both are hostage to two slippery definitions:

- **What counts as "AGI"?** A weak "can do most economically valuable remote work" bar is very different from a strong "matches human experts across essentially all cognitive domains, including efficient learning of genuinely novel tasks."
- **What counts as "an LLM" vs. "fundamentally different"?** Frontier systems are already transformers + RL reasoning + tool use + multimodality + agentic scaffolding + memory. If AGI arrives as a transformer core bolted to world-models and symbolic components, is that "an LLM" or "something new"? I treat *transformer/large-scale self-supervised learning as the core engine* as "LLM lineage."

## Factor 1: P(AGI by 2035) ≈ 35–40%

**Pushing it up:**
- Scaling laws have held across ~6 orders of magnitude.
- When pretraining gains looked like they were softening, inference-time/RL reasoning (o1/o3, R1) opened a *new* scaling axis — the paradigm keeps finding fresh oxygen.
- ~$100B+ in capital and most of the field's talent aimed directly at this.
- Lab leaders project 2–7 years (though they're incentivized to).

**Pushing it down:**
- "AGI" is a high bar, and current systems still show stubborn failures: hallucination, unreliability over long horizons, weak continual learning, poor sample efficiency.
- Benchmark progress likely overstates *general* intelligence (contamination, Goodharting).
- High-quality data limits; energy/compute/chip constraints.
- AI has a long history of confidently wrong 10-year predictions.

I land around **~37%**.

## Factor 2: P(LLM-lineage | AGI by 2035) ≈ 75%

The key argument: **a short timeline favors the incumbent paradigm.** A "fundamentally different architecture" (e.g., LeCun-style world models/JEPA, neuro-symbolic systems) would need time to be invented, validated, *and* scaled — hard to do all that and win the race within ~10 years. If AGI is close, it most likely grew out of what's already dominant.

What lowers this below ~90%: serious researchers (LeCun, Marcus) think the current path is insufficient *and* that AGI is reachable other ways — so a non-trivial slice of near-term AGI worlds run through new ideas. Plus the boundary problem: heavily modified transformer systems might reasonably get labeled "fundamentally different."

I land around **~75%**.

## Combining

0.37 × 0.75 ≈ **0.28**

## The honest caveat

That 28% is far less robust than it looks, because definitions swamp everything:
- **Weak AGI definition + generous "LLM" definition** → could be **40–50%**
- **Strict AGI + strict "pure scaling, no major new components" definition** → could be **10–15%**

My own read on the Marcus quote: "Game over" is rhetorical overreach — every predicted wall (data, pretraining saturation) has so far been at least partially routed around. But his defensible core claim — that *pure* LLM scaling alone may not suffice and that important new ingredients are needed — is genuinely plausible. The most likely AGI-by-2035 scenario, in my view, is neither "pure LLM" nor "fundamentally different," but a transformer-*centered* hybrid — which is exactly why the answer depends so heavily on which side of that fuzzy line you place it.

**Single number: ~28%.**

Short answer up front: **~30%.** Here's how I get there and why the number is so sensitive to definitions.

## The two things that have to both be true

This is really a conjunction, so I'll decompose it:

**P(AGI by 2035 at all) × P(it's LLM-based | AGI by 2035)**

### 1. P(AGI achieved by 2035) ≈ 35%

Using a reasonably demanding definition (matches or exceeds competent humans across most cognitive work, including learning new tasks and acting reliably over long horizons):

*Pushing higher:*
- Genuinely unprecedented progress 2020–2025 (GPT-3 → reasoning models → agents)
- Enormous capital, compute, and talent concentration
- Reasoning-via-RL (o-series, etc.) cracked a problem many thought needed new ideas

*Pushing lower:*
- AGI has been "10–20 years away" for 60 years
- Unsolved hard problems: reliability/hallucination, continual learning, robust out-of-distribution reasoning, long-horizon autonomy
- Possible data and scaling walls; energy/chip constraints
- "Most cognitive work" is a high bar once you include the messy, embodied, long-tail stuff

Reasonable people land anywhere from 10% to 60% here. I'll use ~35%.

### 2. P(LLM-based | AGI by 2035) ≈ 80%

This is the crux of your actual question, and it's where the **continuity argument** dominates:

- The shorter the timeline, the more any AGI *must* be a descendant of what works now — there isn't time for a brand-new paradigm to be invented, validated, and scaled to AGI within ~10 years.
- Every recent capability jump (reasoning, tool use, multimodality, agency) was built *on top of* the transformer/self-supervised-pretraining foundation, not by replacing it.
- A fundamentally different paradigm (LeCun-style world models, neurosymbolic systems) is plausible, but most likely outcomes are **hybrids with an LLM at the core** — which I'd still count as "LLM-descended."

The big caveat: "LLM vs. fundamentally different" is genuinely blurry (a Ship-of-Theseus problem). If you set a strict bar for what counts as "still an LLM," this number drops.

## Combining

0.35 × 0.80 ≈ **0.28 → I'll call it ~30%.**

## What would move this number

- **Looser AGI definition** ("does most digital knowledge work"): pushes toward ~40%.
- **Stricter AGI definition** (robust human-level general intelligence): toward ~20%.
- **Counting only "pure" LLMs** as LLMs: lower, maybe ~20%, because the winning system will almost certainly be a hybrid.

## On Marcus specifically

His narrow claim — "scaling pure LLMs won't smoothly produce AGI" — is defensible and shared by many serious researchers. But his rhetorical leap to "game over" is overconfident: the field has already absorbed his critiques by bolting on RL-based reasoning, tools, and memory rather than abandoning the paradigm. The most likely path to AGI (if it arrives by 2035) is *neither* "pure scaling works" *nor* "throw it all out" — it's LLMs as the substrate inside a larger, partly-novel system. That fuzzy middle is exactly why a clean "LLM vs. not" probability is hard to pin down.

**Best single number: ~30%**, held loosely (I'd defend anything from 20% to 35%).

rows.answer:
Short answer: **roughly 30%** — but that single number hides two very different questions, and most of the uncertainty isn't actually about LLMs vs. alternatives.

## Decomposing the question

The probability factors into two parts:

**P(AGI achieved by 2035 via LLMs) = P(AGI by 2035) × P(it's LLM-based | AGI by 2035)**

**1. Will *any* AGI arrive by 2035? (~35%, wide error bars)**

This is the dominant uncertainty, and where reasonable people disagree most.
- *Pushing higher:* unprecedented capital (hundreds of billions in compute), multiple well-funded labs racing, fast 2020–2025 capability gains, and a fresh source of progress in inference-time reasoning and agentic systems after pretraining scaling started to saturate.
- *Pushing lower:* "AGI" is a high bar (robust out-of-distribution generalization, reliable long-horizon agency, continual learning, causal reasoning — all still weak). AI has a long history of underestimating the "last 10%," and ten years is short for a qualitative leap.

I'd put this at ~30–40% for a genuinely broad, human-level system. Lab leaders would say 50–80%; Marcus and many academics would say 5–15%.

**2. If AGI does arrive by 2035, is it LLM-based? (~75%)**

Here's the key insight that cuts *against* the Marcus framing: **short timelines strongly favor the incumbent paradigm.** The transformer is from 2017 and took ~5–8 years to mature into today's systems. A genuinely new architecture starting from scratch now would struggle to overtake it and reach AGI by 2035. So *conditional on near-term AGI*, the LLM lineage is the heavy favorite — I'd say 70–80%.

Combining: 0.38 × 0.75 ≈ **~28–30%.**

## The hidden crux: what counts as "an LLM"?

This matters more than it looks. Today's frontier systems already aren't pure next-token predictors — they bolt on RL, tool use, retrieval, chain-of-thought, multimodality, mixture-of-experts. The AGI of 2033, even if descended from LLMs, will likely add world models, better memory, and possibly neurosymbolic components.

- Under a **broad** definition ("built around large transformer LMs, even heavily modified"), the conditional probability is high (~75–80%) and the headline number is ~30%.
- Under a **strict** definition ("essentially scaled-up LLMs with no fundamental new ingredient"), it drops toward ~50%, and the headline falls to ~20%.

Marcus and the labs are often arguing past each other on exactly this boundary — a hybrid system that crosses the AGI line lets *both* sides claim victory.

## On the "Game over" framing

That tweet is overconfident in the skeptical direction, the same way lab hype is overconfident in the optimistic direction. A single release or disappointing result almost never resolves a question this large; Marcus has declared variants of "game over" repeatedly across the scaling era. The honest position is that this is genuinely unresolved.

## My bottom line

**~30%**, driven mostly by the coin-flip-ish odds that AGI arrives at all by 2035 — *not* by skepticism about LLMs specifically. Conditional on AGI by 2035, I think LLM-descended architectures are clearly the favorite (~3:1). If you adopt a strict AGI definition, shade me down to ~20%; a loose one, up to ~40%.

(One caveat on my own calibration: I can't fully exclude that my read of the current paradigm is shaped by it being the dominant one I was trained around — genuine paradigm shifts look obvious only in hindsight.)

rows.answer:
Two distinct claims are bundled in your question, and separating them clarifies the estimate:

1. **Will AGI arrive by 2035 at all?**
2. **If it does, will it come from the LLM lineage rather than a fundamentally different architecture?**

The probability you want is roughly the product of these.

## P(AGI by 2035)

**Pushing higher:** Unprecedented capital and talent concentration; scaling laws have largely held; new scaling axes opened up recently (test-time compute / RL on verifiable rewards) beyond just pretraining; people chronically underestimate compounding progress; 10 years is long at the current pace.

**Pushing lower:** Deep unsolved problems remain—reliable long-horizon reasoning, continual learning, robust generalization, hallucination, genuine grounding. Benchmark gains may overstate real-world capability. Possible data/scaling walls. And AI has a long history of "20 years away" predictions that didn't land. "AGI" is also a high, fuzzy bar.

My estimate: **~25–35%**, call it **30%** — with the caveat that this swings a lot depending on definition. (Lab leaders would say 60–80%; committed skeptics like Marcus/LeCun, under 15%.)

## P(LLM-lineage | AGI by 2035)

The key insight is a timing argument: **fundamentally new paradigms take time to invent, validate, and scale.** If AGI is only a decade out, that short window structurally favors the paradigm that *already* owns the compute, data pipelines, capital, and talent—transformers plus their extensions (RL, multimodality, agentic scaffolding, memory, test-time reasoning). For something genuinely different (world-model/JEPA approaches, neuro-symbolic, etc.) to *win by 2035*, it would have to emerge soon and overtake an entrenched paradigm with a massive head start.

Important caveat: frontier systems are already "LLM+", not pure next-token predictors. I'm counting transformer-core + scaffolding as "via LLMs." If you define LLM narrowly, this number drops.

My estimate: **~75–80%**.

## Joint estimate

$$0.30 \times 0.78 \approx 0.22$$

**My best single number: ~20%** (roughly 1 in 5), with wide error bars.

## A useful asymmetry to note

If LLMs plateau, that *both* lowers P(AGI by 2035) *and* raises the chance any eventual AGI is non-LLM. But because new paradigms rarely mature fast enough, a plateau mostly just makes AGI-by-2035 less likely overall rather than handing the win to a different architecture within the window. This is why Marcus can be right that "LLMs aren't the royal road" *and* it can still be true that **if** AGI shows up by 2035, it most likely came from something LLM-shaped—those aren't contradictory.

What would move my number: a stricter AGI definition → lower; a looser "transformative AI" definition → higher joint and near-certain LLM lineage; clear evidence of continued RL/test-time scaling gains → higher.

**Short answer: ~25%.**

The question bundles two distinct claims, so it's worth separating them:

**P(AGI via LLMs by 2035) = P(AGI by 2035) × P(it's LLM-descended | AGI by 2035)**

## P(AGI by 2035) ≈ 0.35

This is the bigger source of uncertainty, and it depends heavily on how strictly you define "AGI" (broad economic-substitute definition vs. "matches or beats humans at virtually *all* cognitive tasks").

- Unprecedented capital/talent/compute (hundreds of billions deployed)
- Scaling laws have held better than skeptics predicted for ~6 years
- A *new* scaling axis (test-time compute / RL on reasoning) is producing gains just as pretraining gains soften
- Strong commercial incentive accelerating everything

Factors pulling down:
- "AGI" is a very high bar; current systems are "jagged" (superhuman in spots, surprisingly dumb in others)
- Unsolved hard problems: reliable reasoning, continual learning, genuine agency, hallucination, robust world models
- Data wall for high-quality pretraining text
- AI's long history of overconfident timelines and winters

Expert surveys tend to cluster the *median* later (≈2040s) for strong definitions; lab leaders cluster it much earlier (2026–2030). I split the difference toward ~0.35 for a 10-year window.

## P(LLM-descended | AGI by 2035) ≈ 0.7

Here's the key insight: **the two halves are correlated.** A short timeline *selects for* the current paradigm, because a genuinely new architecture (LeCun's world models, neurosymbolic, brain-inspired, something un-invented) would need to be conceived, scaled, and matured within ~10 years—hard if it isn't already showing strong promise. So *conditional on AGI arriving soon*, it's more likely to be an evolution of today's transformer/pretraining/RL stack than a revolution.

## Combining

0.35 × 0.7 ≈ **0.25**, with a defensible range of **0.15–0.35** depending mostly on how you define AGI.

## On the Marcus claim

My estimate means I think it's *more likely than not* that either AGI doesn't arrive by 2035 or it arrives via something Marcus wouldn't call "just an LLM." So I'm directionally sympathetic to his skepticism that LLMs are *sufficient* as-is.

But "Game over" is overconfident. A ~25% chance of LLM-lineage AGI within a decade is not "game over"—it's one of the highest-probability single pathways available, and the line between "scaled-up LLM" and "fundamentally different architecture" is blurry enough that hybrid systems could let *both* Marcus and the scaling optimists claim they were right. The honest position is wide uncertainty, not a victory lap in either direction.

rows.answer:
**Short answer: ~30%.**

This is a joint probability that's worth decomposing, because most of the disagreement lives in the definitions and in two separate questions.

**The hidden definitional crux.** "Via LLMs" vs. "fundamentally different architecture" is fuzzy. Today's frontier systems already aren't pure next-token predictors—they bundle in RLHF, tool use, retrieval, multimodality, agentic scaffolding, and test-time reasoning (o1/o3-style). So the real question is: does the *current paradigm* (large transformer-based neural nets, scaled and augmented) get there, or do we need something categorically different (LeCun's world models / JEPA, neurosymbolic systems, brain-like architectures)? I'll interpret "LLMs" generously as "the transformer-descended lineage, even heavily modified."

**Component 1 — P(AGI by 2035 at all): ~40%**
- *For:* Genuinely surprising progress (GPT-3 → GPT-4 → reasoning models); a new scaling axis (test-time compute) opened up just as pretraining returns flattened; ~hundreds of billions in capital and most top talent pointed at this; the paradigm has proven shockingly general.
- *Against:* "AGI" is a high bar (robust generalization, continual learning, reliability, genuine novelty—not just benchmark scores). Real walls exist: data limits, hallucination, brittle planning, sample inefficiency. Ten years is short, and the gap between "impressive demo" and "matches humans across most cognitive work" is historically underestimated.

**Component 2 — P(it's the LLM lineage | AGI by 2035): ~78%**
- Short timelines strongly favor the incumbent. A fundamentally different architecture would need years to mature, and no current alternative (world models, neurosymbolic) is close to frontier performance. If AGI arrives *soon*, it almost has to come from extending what already works.
- The discount from 100%: people like LeCun explicitly bet LLMs are a dead end and are building alternatives; it's plausible (~20%) that the thing that finally works is different enough to count as a new paradigm. Also, breakthroughs tend to get *absorbed* into the dominant paradigm, which actually pushes this number up.

**Combining:** 0.40 × 0.78 ≈ **0.31**, so I'll say **~30%**.

**Where reasonable people land elsewhere:**
- Marcus would put this far lower (<10%), because he thinks LLMs fundamentally can't get there *and* that true AGI is further off than the hype suggests.
- A lab optimist (Altman/Amodei flavor) would say 50–70%, treating continued scaling + reasoning + agents as basically sufficient.

My 30% reflects two judgments: AGI by 2035 is plausible but clearly not the base case (the biggest source of downside), while *conditional* on it happening that soon, it's probably LLM-lineage by default.

One caveat worth stating plainly: this estimate is more sensitive to how you define "AGI" and "LLM" than to any empirical fact, so treat the precise number as soft. The Marcus post is also partly a vibe/marketing skirmish (note the xAI tag)—a single underwhelming model tells you little about a 10-year trajectory in either direction.
